title: India-US Interim Trade Deal Nears Finalisation; USTR Greer to Visit New Delhi on June 23–24
summary: India and the United States are in the final stages of an interim bilateral trade deal, described as requiring only 'final touches'. US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer is scheduled to visit New Delhi on June 23–24, 2025 to conclude negotiations. The deal is being closely watched amid broader G7 discussions on trade and tariffs, with Prime Minister Modi and President Trump engaging on the sidelines.
exam_angle: Relevant for GS Paper 2 (India-US relations, bilateral trade negotiations) and GS Paper 3 (trade policy, tariffs). Tests understanding of India's trade diplomacy and WTO-plus bilateral arrangements.
key_facts: USTR Greer visit: June 23–24, New Delhi
Deal described as needing only 'final touches'
Context: G7 summit discussions on trade and tariffs
India may seek a sunset clause in the US trade pact amid tariff concerns

title: India May Seek Sunset Clause in US Trade Pact Amid Tariff Concerns
summary: India is reportedly considering demanding a sunset clause in the prospective trade agreement with the United States, which would allow the deal to be reviewed or terminated after a fixed period. This reflects India's caution about locking into long-term tariff commitments, particularly given US protectionist tendencies under the Trump administration. The clause would provide a built-in review mechanism to renegotiate terms as geopolitical and economic conditions evolve.
exam_angle: Relevant for GS Paper 3 (International Trade, trade agreements) and GS Paper 2 (India's foreign trade policy). A sunset clause is an important trade law concept frequently tested.
key_facts: Sunset clause: a provision allowing a treaty/law to expire after a set period unless renewed
India's concern: long-term tariff commitments under a changing US trade policy
Context: ongoing India-US interim trade deal negotiations
Related to broader US tariff hikes under Trump administration (2025)

title: Supreme Court Issues Order on Right to Walk on Footpaths; Congress Seeks Fundamental Right to Vote
summary: The Supreme Court of India passed an order recognising the right to walk on footpaths, citing a significant surge in pedestrian deaths in India due to encroachments and inadequate infrastructure. The court connected the issue to the right to life under Article 21 of the Constitution. Following this ruling, the Congress party demanded recognition of the fundamental right to vote, drawing a parallel to the court's expansive interpretation of Article 21.
exam_angle: Tests knowledge of Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty) and the Supreme Court's expansive interpretation of fundamental rights. Also relevant for urban governance and road safety policy.
key_facts: Article 21: Right to Life and Personal Liberty
SC linked right to walk on footpaths to Article 21
India has one of the world's highest rates of pedestrian road fatalities
Congress demanded the right to vote be recognised as a fundamental right post-ruling

title: Police Do Not Need Court Permission for Further Investigation After Filing Final Report: Supreme Court
summary: The Supreme Court of India clarified that the police do not require court permission to conduct further investigation in a case even after filing the final report (chargesheet) under the Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC). The court held that under Section 173(8) of CrPC, the power of further investigation is an inherent statutory right of the police and does not require prior judicial sanction. This ruling has significant implications for criminal procedural law.
exam_angle: Directly relevant to GS Paper 2 (Polity – Judiciary, Criminal Justice System) and for Law Optional. Tests understanding of CrPC provisions, especially Section 173 and the distinction between further investigation and re-investigation.
key_facts: Section 173(8) CrPC: allows further investigation after filing of final report
SC ruling: police do not need court permission for further investigation
Distinction: further investigation (by police) vs. reinvestigation (court-ordered)
Final report = chargesheet filed under Section 173 CrPC

title: US and Iran Begin Nuclear Talks in Switzerland; Iran Sets Condition of Lebanon Ceasefire
summary: The United States and Iran commenced fresh negotiations in Switzerland aimed at resolving the Iranian nuclear issue and broader geopolitical tensions. Iran stated it would not enter into peace talks with the US unless the war in Lebanon (involving Israeli operations) is stopped, linking the two conflicts diplomatically. Simultaneously, US President Trump threatened to 'hit harder' if a deal is not reached, to which Iran responded with a warning to 'be careful', indicating significant diplomatic brinkmanship.
exam_angle: Relevant for GS Paper 2 (IR – West Asia geopolitics, nuclear non-proliferation). Tests understanding of Iran's nuclear programme, the JCPOA context, and US–Iran–Israel strategic triangle.
key_facts: Venue of talks: Switzerland
Iran's condition: cessation of Lebanon conflict before broader peace talks
Trump threatened further action; Iran warned of consequences
Context: JCPOA (Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action) breakdown and ongoing Iran nuclear standoff

title: India's Dependence on Services Exports Growing; Structural Shift in Export Composition
summary: A Business Standard analysis highlights that India's export composition is undergoing a structural shift, with services exports constituting an increasingly large share compared to merchandise exports. India's services exports—led by IT, business process management, and financial services—have been growing faster than goods exports, reflecting the country's comparative advantage in knowledge-intensive sectors. This shift has implications for India's current account balance and trade policy priorities.
exam_angle: Relevant for GS Paper 3 (Indian Economy – Balance of Payments, services sector, export policy). Tests understanding of India's trade structure, current account deficit, and the role of the services sector in GDP.
key_facts: India's services exports are growing faster than merchandise exports
Key services: IT/ITeS, business services, financial services
Services exports help offset India's merchandise trade deficit
India is among the world's top exporters of commercial services

title: A New Chapter in India's Nuclear Journey: PIB Highlights Progress in Civil Nuclear Energy
summary: The Press Information Bureau (PIB) published a detailed note marking a new phase in India's civilian nuclear energy programme. The update covers developments in indigenous nuclear technology, expansion of nuclear power capacity under the Department of Atomic Energy (DAE), and progress on Pressurised Heavy Water Reactors (PHWRs) and Fast Breeder Reactors. India aims to significantly scale up nuclear power generation as part of its clean energy transition commitments.
exam_angle: Relevant for GS Paper 3 (Sci-Tech – Nuclear energy, India's energy policy). Tests knowledge of India's three-stage nuclear programme, key institutions (DAE, NPCIL, BARC), and India's nuclear doctrine.
key_facts: Nodal body: Department of Atomic Energy (DAE), under the Prime Minister's Office
India's three-stage nuclear programme: PHWRs → Fast Breeder Reactors → Thorium-based reactors
Key entities: NPCIL (Nuclear Power Corporation of India Ltd), BARC, IGCAR
India has the world's largest thorium reserves, central to Stage 3 of the programme

title: West Asia Crisis Pulls Global Startup Funding Down 43% Year-on-Year Since March 2025
summary: Global startup funding has declined by 43% year-on-year since March 2025, with the ongoing West Asia (Middle East) crisis cited as a key contributing factor. The geopolitical instability has elevated risk aversion among venture capital and private equity investors, leading to a sharp contraction in deal volumes and valuations. Indian startups have also been affected, though the domestic market has shown relative resilience compared to global peers.
exam_angle: Relevant for GS Paper 3 (Economy – startup ecosystem, venture capital, impact of geopolitics on financial markets). Tests understanding of the India-specific startup policy environment and global capital flow dynamics.
key_facts: Startup funding declined 43% YoY since March 2025
Primary cause cited: West Asia geopolitical crisis
Impact: higher investor risk aversion, lower deal volumes globally
India's startup ecosystem: third largest globally by number of unicorns

title: Government's Sovereign AI Model Strategy Faces Scrutiny; Debate on Viability of India-Specific AI
summary: A Business Standard analysis raises questions about the Indian government's push for sovereign AI models, arguing that developing entirely indigenous large language models (LLMs) may not be the most efficient strategy given the resource intensity of AI model training. The debate centres on India's IndiaAI Mission and whether the focus should be on fine-tuning and deploying existing global models for Indian languages and use-cases rather than building from scratch. Experts suggest a hybrid approach balancing sovereignty with practicality.
exam_angle: Relevant for GS Paper 3 (Sci-Tech – Artificial Intelligence policy, IndiaAI Mission). Tests awareness of India's AI governance framework, Digital India initiatives, and the tension between technological sovereignty and efficiency.
key_facts: IndiaAI Mission: approved by Cabinet in March 2024 with ₹10,371.92 crore outlay
Sovereign AI: government-controlled AI models trained on domestic data
Key concern: high cost of training foundational models from scratch
Alternative strategy: fine-tuning global models for Indian languages (e.g., 22 scheduled languages)

title: US President Trump Removes Anthropic from National Security Threat List; AI Policy Shift Noted
summary: US President Donald Trump reportedly no longer views Anthropic, the AI safety company behind the Claude AI models, as a national security threat. This marks a significant shift in US AI governance posture, potentially easing regulatory scrutiny on Anthropic's operations and international partnerships. Separately, Anthropic's 'Fable 5' AI model was briefly restricted and then withdrawn, raising questions about AI safety protocols and model governance.
exam_angle: Relevant for GS Paper 3 (Sci-Tech – AI governance, US technology policy). Tests awareness of global AI regulation trends and their implications for India's AI partnerships and policy.
key_facts: Anthropic: US-based AI safety company, creator of 'Claude' LLM series
Trump administration reversed its classification of Anthropic as a national security concern
'Fable 5' model: briefly released and then restricted/withdrawn by Anthropic
Context: broader US AI export control and national security review of AI firms

title: 12th International Day of Yoga 2026 Celebrated by AIIA with Diverse Wellness Activities
summary: The All India Institute of Ayurveda (AIIA), New Delhi, celebrated the 12th International Day of Yoga with enthusiastic participation featuring diverse wellness activities including yoga sessions, workshops on traditional Indian medicine, and awareness programmes. International Day of Yoga is observed annually on June 21, following the UN General Assembly's adoption of the resolution in 2014 at India's initiative. The 2025 theme continues India's global promotion of yoga as an intangible cultural heritage.
exam_angle: Relevant for GS Paper 1 (Culture – Indian cultural heritage, yoga as soft power) and GS Paper 2 (IR – India's multilateral diplomacy). Tests knowledge of the origin of International Day of Yoga and AIIA's mandate.
key_facts: International Day of Yoga: observed on June 21 every year
UN Resolution: adopted on December 11, 2014 (A/RES/69/131), proposed by PM Modi
First International Day of Yoga: June 21, 2015
AIIA: All India Institute of Ayurveda, New Delhi; under Ministry of AYUSH

title: Modi-Trump G7 Meeting: Trade, Tariffs, and Strategic Reset Dominate Agenda
summary: Prime Minister Narendra Modi and US President Donald Trump held discussions on the sidelines of the G7 Summit, with trade relations, tariffs, and strategic bilateral trust forming the core agenda. The meeting is seen as an attempt to reset the Modi-Trump relationship after friction over US reciprocal tariffs imposed in 2025. India and the US are simultaneously negotiating an interim trade deal, and the G7 engagement provided a high-level political push to accelerate the process.
exam_angle: Relevant for GS Paper 2 (IR – India-US strategic partnership, G7, bilateral trade diplomacy). Tests understanding of G7's composition, India's engagement with the grouping, and current trade tensions.
key_facts: G7 members: USA, UK, Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan (+ EU as non-enumerated member)
India is not a G7 member but is regularly invited as a guest/outreach partner
Context: US imposed reciprocal tariffs on India in April 2025 (26% baseline)
Key issues: trade deal, tariffs, technology transfer, and defence cooperation
